device_description: The Laparoscopic Stapler X100 is a single-use, sterile medical device designed to create endoscopic stapled anastomoses during minimally invasive surgical procedures. It consists of a polymer handle with an integrated trigger mechanism, an articulating metal shaft for precise positioning, and removable staple cartridges made of titanium and synthetic polymers. The stapling mechanism clamps the tissue and deploys staggered rows of staples while simultaneously cutting between them, enabling safe transection and reconnection of soft tissue.
intended_purpose: The Laparoscopic Stapler X100 is intended for use in minimally invasive laparoscopic surgical procedures to transect and staple soft tissue of the gastrointestinal tract. It is intended for use by trained surgeons in hospital operating rooms.
indications_for_use: Transection and stapling of soft tissue during laparoscopic bowel resection
Stapled anastomosis during laparoscopic colorectal surgery
contraindications: Patients with known hypersensitivity to titanium or device polymers
Patients whose anatomy is incompatible with laparoscopic stapler introduction
target_patient_population: Adult patients undergoing elective laparoscopic gastrointestinal surgery assessed by a surgeon as suitable for staple anastomosis and transection.
intended_user_profile: Trained healthcare professionals, including general surgeons and bariatric surgeons, familiar with minimally invasive laparoscopic stapling techniques.
eu_mdr_classification_and_rule: EU Class IIb under EU MDR 2017/745, Rule 10
uk_mdr_classification_and_rule: UK Class IIb under UK MDR 2002 (as amended 2024), Rule 10
uk_responsible_person: MedCorp UK Ltd, 123 London Road, London, UK
market_history: The Laparoscopic Stapler X100 was first launched and CE marked in 2015, with prior FDA 510(k) clearance (K123456) obtained in July 2014. Annual cumulative global sales exceed 200,000 units. Since launch, significant post-market events include two major design enhancements in 2017 and 2020 addressing reported misfire incidences, the implementation of a comprehensive surgeon training program in 2021, and introduction in the Asia-Pacific region in 2022.
